hub_subtitle: Business English para seguros, reuniões e entrevistas — e o desbloqueio da fala, do primeiro dia ao último

lesson.menu_title: Who, What, Where, When, How -- Asking the Right Questions
lesson.menu_desc: A aula em que ela deixa de responder e passa a PERGUNTAR. Novembro chegou: a viagem a Miami que a aula 6 anunciou acontece hoje, e ela vai sozinha. Seis palavras do aeroporto (gate, flight, departure, arrival, reservation, spell) e as cinco palavrinhas que abrem qualquer porta -- who, what, where, when, how -- com a única regra que importa: depois da pergunta vem o ajudante (is ou does), nunca o sujeito. O português deixa dizer "onde o portão está?"; o inglês vira: "Where is the gate?" -- quatro palavras, e alguém aponta. -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 8 -- Who, What, Where, When, How
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Paula Morales | Lesson 8 -- Who, What, Where, When, How
lesson.grammar_point: question words (who / what / where / when / how) -- wh-questions with the verb to be and with do/does

listenings.text: Hi, Paula. David here. Your trip to Miami is next week, and I have one thing to tell you — and it is not about the flight. The flight is on Monday. The departure from São Paulo is at ten in the morning, and the arrival in Miami is at six in the evening. The gate is on your boarding pass, but it changes — so look at the screen, always. You have a reservation at the hotel, three nights, and the office is ten minutes from there. Now the important part. This time you travel alone. People are going to ask you things: What is your name? How do you spell it? Where are you from? And you are going to want to ask things too. Here is my advice, and it is the only one I have: ask the question. Don't build a beautiful sentence in your head first. Where is the gate? Four words. That is a complete question, and it works. See you on Monday, at nine.

Good morning, Paula. Sofia here, about Miami. Two things, and then go and pack. One: the work is the easy part. You know the accounts, you know the numbers, and in the Miami office everybody speaks slowly for you — I asked them to. Two, and this is the real one: the airport. You are alone, and you are going to be afraid at the airport. I know, because I was. Twenty years ago, my first day in New York: I did not ask anything. I walked for one hour, lost, because I did not want to say a wrong sentence. Don't do that, Paula. Here is the secret nobody tells you: at an airport, nobody wants a beautiful sentence. They want a question. Where is the gate? Four words, and somebody points. That is all. Ask the question, and let it be ugly. See you in Miami.

reading.rtitle: Your Trip to Miami — Travel Desk, Zurich Brazil
paras: Paula, here is everything for your trip. We write it as questions, because these are the questions you are going to ask. Read it on the plane.

When is the flight? Your flight is on Monday. The departure from São Paulo is at ten in the morning, and the arrival in Miami is at six in the evening — Miami time. Where is the gate? We don't know, and on Monday morning nobody knows. The gate is on the screen at the airport, and it changes. Look at the screen, not at this page.

Where do you sleep? You have a reservation at the hotel, for three nights, and the office is ten minutes from there. The reservation is in your name. They are going to ask you one question at the hotel: How do you spell your last name? The answer is M-O-R-A-L-E-S. Say it slowly, letter by letter. Everybody asks this, and nobody thinks it is strange.

Who is your contact in Miami? Sofia. And this is Sofia, in her own words: "Paula, the office is the easy part. The airport is the hard part, and it is hard for everybody. Nobody at an airport wants a perfect sentence. They want a question. Ask it — four words is a question. Where is the gate? Somebody points, and you go."
reading.source: Zurich Brazil — internal travel desk page

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The page says Paula's flight is late, and she has no reservation in Miami.
The page is Paula's trip, written as the questions she is going to ask — and it says the gate is on the screen, not on the page.
The page explains that Paula travels to Miami with her manager.

items: The departure is at ten in the morning, and the arrival in Miami is at six in the evening.
t
The text says it: "The departure from São Paulo is at ten in the morning, and the arrival in Miami is at six in the evening."
The travel page tells Paula which gate her flight leaves from.
f
No. It says the opposite: "We don't know, and on Monday morning nobody knows. The gate is on the screen at the airport, and it changes."
Paula has a reservation at the hotel for three nights.
t
"You have a reservation at the hotel, for three nights, and the office is ten minutes from there."
At the hotel they are going to ask Paula to spell her last name.
t
"They are going to ask you one question at the hotel: How do you spell your last name?" And the text says nobody thinks it is strange.
Sofia says Paula needs a perfect sentence at the airport.
f
She says the opposite: "Nobody at an airport wants a perfect sentence. They want a question. Ask it — four words is a question."

bank.label: At the airport / No aeroporto
items: Where is the gate?
What time is the departure?
When does the flight arrive?
Excuse me, where is my flight?
How do I get to the hotel?

bank.label: At the hotel and at the office / No hotel e no escritório
items: I have a reservation. My name is Paula Morales.
How do you spell that?
Who is the manager here?
What do you do here?
Can you repeat that, please?

items.situation: You are at the airport and you don't know where to go. Ask the question — and remember: four words are enough.
tips: Excuse me, where is gate B7?

items.situation: You want to know what time the plane leaves. Ask with the verb to be — this one has no do or does.
You want to know what time the plane lands in Miami. Careful: this one has a normal verb, so it needs the helper.
tips: What time does the flight arrive in Miami?

items.situation: The receptionist says a name and you don't catch it. Ask her to say it letter by letter.
tips: Sorry, how do you spell your name?

items.situation: You arrive at the hotel. Say why you are there, and give your name — before anybody asks.
tips: I have a reservation. My name is Paula Morales.
Hi. I have a reservation for three nights.

items.situation: First morning at the Miami office. You want to know who runs the commercial team. Ask.
